content.problemStatement:
Your best friend has a very interesting necklace with nnn pearls. On each of the pearls of the necklace there is an integer. However, your friend wants to modify the necklace a bit and asks you for help. She wants to move the first pearl kkk spots to the left (and do so with all other pearls).

For example: if the necklace was originally 1,5,3,4,21, 5, 3, 4, 21,5,3,4,2 and k=2k = 2k=2, now it becomes 3,4,2,1,53, 4, 2, 1, 53,4,2,1,5.

Help your best friend determine how the necklace will look after the modification.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input, the first containing two integers n,kn, kn,k.
The second line of each test case contains nnn integers a1,a2,...,ana_1, a_2, ..., a_na1​,a2​,...,an​ representing the integers on the pearls starting from the first one.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line nnn integers representing the necklace after modification.
constraints: Sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 300000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers n and k (the number of pearls and the left rotation distance). The second line contains n space‑separated integers a1 … an, the values on the pearls in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with n space‑separated integers representing the necklace after rotating it left by k positions.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: rotating left by 3 moves the first three elements (1,5,3) to the end, giving 4 2 1 5 3. Second test case: rotating left by 5 moves the first five elements to the end, resulting in 2 10 1 2 9 8.
k = 0 means no rotation; the array stays unchanged.
Rotating left by 2 moves the first two elements (-5,0) to the end, yielding 5 -5 0.

hidden.label: general case with negatives and zeros

solution.approach: For each test case we need to perform a left rotation by k positions. Because rotating by n yields the original array, we first replace k with k % n. The resulting order is the suffix starting at index k followed by the prefix of length k. We can output the elements in that order without creating a new array, using two simple loops. The algorithm runs in O(n) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total n) where total n is the sum of lengths of all test cases (≤ 3·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input storage.
